frontmatter.description: A practical overview of Knowledge Graphs and Ontologies in the AI Agent era — the concepts, internal components, construction methodologies, and the latest techniques combining them with LLMs and agents. This post was written with the help of Gemini Deep Research and Claude Code, based on publicly available sources.
frontmatter.title: Knowledge Graph and Ontology: Core Concepts

frontmatter.description: This post explains, from the perspective of a single-node Python developer, why Hadoop appeared first, why Spark followed, and why Kafka became the layer in front of them. It ties together HDFS and YARN, Spark's DAG and Catalyst, and Kafka's zero-copy and exactly-once patterns in one flow. Drafted with Gemini Deep Research and refined with GPT-5.4.
frontmatter.title: Beyond Single-Node Data Processing

frontmatter.description: This post documents the essential things you need to know when implementing vector search with pgvector's HNSW index. It covers the mechanics of iterative_scan, filtering strategies, and performance optimization. All text and images were created with Claude Code.
frontmatter.title: pgvector HNSW: A Practical Guide

frontmatter.description: This post analyzes how coding agents work internally. We compare the orchestration pipelines, memory structures, sandboxing, and permission control mechanisms of major coding agents including Claude Code, OpenCode, oh-my-opencode (Sisyphus), Codex, and OpenClaw.
frontmatter.title: Internal Architecture of Coding Agents

frontmatter.description: A step-by-step look at the FLUX.2 [dev] 32B model architecture from Black Forest Labs, comparing it with FLUX.1 across text encoders, Transformer structure, VAE, and sampling process. This post was written based on analysis of the flux.2[dev] architecture performed by Claude Code.
frontmatter.title: FLUX.2 [dev] Architecture

frontmatter.description: Go is widely loved in modern software development, as core cloud-native infrastructure tools like Docker, Kubernetes, and Prometheus are all written in Go. Go pursues both the performance of C++ and the productivity of Python, enabling intuitive code writing without complex abstractions. Go's core design principles are 'simplicity' and 'pragmatism'...
frontmatter.title: Golang Basics Guide

frontmatter.description: A quick overview and summary of key points from the Qwen3-VL Technical Report. Qwen3-VL was designed to address problems such as catastrophic forgetting where text processing ability degrades while improving vision capabilities, and context window limitations or degraded positional encoding resolution when handling long videos or large documents.
frontmatter.title: Qwen3-VL Technical Report Review

frontmatter.description: Notes on various dev infrastructure topics I've studied. Talos Linux: A modern, immutable, and minimal OS designed for running Kubernetes. API-based management: The standout feature. No SSH access or shell provided. All configuration and management is done via gRPC API through the `talosctl` CLI. Immutable: The OS itself runs from a read-only file system (SquashFS) loaded into memory...
frontmatter.title: Random Topics in Infrastructure

frontmatter.description: When dealing with large volumes of data of any kind, you will inevitably encounter search-related tasks at some point. While there are many ways to implement search, one of the simplest approaches is to store documents in a database like Postgres and implement search by querying Postgres...
frontmatter.title: PostgreSQL Full-Text Search

frontmatter.description: I recently spent a few days working on automating blog post writing with AI, and I'd like to share what I built. It's a pipeline where you simply create a GitHub Issue with a blog post title and brief content, and AI automatically writes the post, translates it, opens a PR, and ultimately deploys it to GitHub Pages.
frontmatter.title: Fully Automating Blog Writing and Deployment with AI

frontmatter.description: This post is a translated and summarized version of Anthropic's engineering blog posts Building effective agents and Multi-agent research system. I translated them for personal study purposes and refined the content after initial translation using Gemini 2.5 Pro.
frontmatter.title: Anthropic's Agent Design Best Practices

frontmatter.description: While langchain and langgraph were previously popular tools for building agents, the OpenAI Agent SDK has recently gained significant traction. Compared to langgraph, the OpenAI Agent SDK has fewer external dependencies, is less complex to use, and stays close to standard Python syntax...
frontmatter.title: OpenAI Agent SDK Basics Guide

frontmatter.description: Visualizing the high-level operation of Gemini CLI and Claude Code using mermaid diagrams. I directly prompted Gemini CLI to visualize the overall flow of gemini cli as a mermaid diagram, then asked it to review whether the result matches its actual logic.
frontmatter.title: Diagrams of Coding Agents

frontmatter.description: AI tasks such as AI-based image generation can take anywhere from minutes to hours to complete, making them extremely difficult to handle within a typical web request-response cycle. Until now, the common approach has been to combine Redis and Celery with the FastAPI framework for asynchronous task processing.
frontmatter.title: Comprehensive Guide to Temporal

frontmatter.description: A review of the ObjectDrop paper presented at ECCV 2024. While many diffusion-based editing models have recently shown impressive performance, generating physically realistic images remains challenging. For instance, object removal models must not only replace pixels occluded by the object but also remove associated shadows and reflections.
frontmatter.title: Paper Review: ObjectDrop (ECCV 2024)

frontmatter.description: This post was written to understand the core concepts of Clean Code, SOLID principles, and Clean Architecture. It is a personal study note summarizing the relevant parts from Gemini 2.5 Pro Deep Research results. While I did review and revise the content, please keep in mind that much of it originates from Deep Research output.
frontmatter.title: Clean Code & Architecture

frontmatter.description: Supabase is an open-source platform that helps you quickly and easily build and deploy backend infrastructure. While providing an API similar to Firebase, it is built on PostgreSQL, allowing you to leverage the strengths of both SQL and open source.
frontmatter.title: Getting Started with Supabase: DB, Auth, Edge Functions

frontmatter.description: An overview of Retrieval-Augmented Generation methodologies. This is a curated summary of results produced using Gemini 2.5 Pro Deep Research. Although I have reviewed and revised the content, please keep in mind that it originates from Deep Research output and read selectively.
frontmatter.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation

frontmatter.description: A review of the Prompt Engineering whitepaper published by Google. Temperature controls the degree of 'creativity' or 'randomness' in the output. Top-K selects from the K words with the highest probability during token generation. Top-P selects from candidates whose cumulative probability reaches P or higher
frontmatter.title: Prompt Engineering Tips

frontmatter.description: A review of the TRELLIS: Structured 3D Latents for Scalable and Versatile 3D Generation paper. Research on 3D generation exists across several directions. Mesh-based methods fall short in detailed appearance modeling, while 3D Gaussian and NeRF-based methods excel in rendering quality but lack in geometry extraction.
frontmatter.title: TRELLIS: Structured 3D Latents for 3D Generation

frontmatter.description: A summary of various topics and papers related to conditional image generation. CFG Scale is a scaling parameter used to control condition-based sampling in diffusion models. It controls the output quality by adjusting the weights of unconditional and conditional samples
frontmatter.title: Conditional Image Generation

frontmatter.description: Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform that provides various automated features for operating, managing, deploying, and scaling multiple containerized applications. This post covers fundamental Kubernetes concepts, kubectl, helm, and more.
frontmatter.title: Comprehensive Guide to Kubernetes

frontmatter.description: Following last year, I'm writing another retrospective this year. When I wrote my first retrospective, expressing my thoughts outwardly felt quite unfamiliar. I remember hesitating over whether to publish it or keep it to myself. But looking back now, that timidity feels a little silly, and I realize how much I've changed in just one year.
frontmatter.title: 2024 Year in Review

frontmatter.description: A quick overview of the evolution of deep generative models over recent years, with reviews of key papers on diffusion and flow matching. One-line summaries of important papers: 2020.06 / DDPM: Learns the reverse process of gradually adding noise to data...
frontmatter.title: Diffusion Models and Flow Matching

frontmatter.description: A review of the Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models paper published in 2020. DDPM is one of the most important papers that shifted the generative modeling field from GAN-centric research to diffusion-based approaches prior to 2020.
frontmatter.title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models

frontmatter.description: Package management is essential in Python development environments. While pip and poetry are the most well-known, projects using uv have been steadily increasing recently. In this post, we will compare the key features and pros and cons of each tool. pip is the oldest and most widely used Python package installation tool...
frontmatter.title: Comparing Python Package Managers: pip, poetry, and uv

frontmatter.description: A summary of the types, tasks, metrics, and related services of generative models. Diffusion models learn to generate new data by progressively restoring data from noise. DDPM learns the process of gradually adding noise to data and then reversing it.
frontmatter.title: An Overview of Generative Art

frontmatter.description: Dart is a programming language developed by Google, used for Flutter app development. It has syntax similar to JavaScript and offers high performance and productivity. It is specifically designed for building client-side applications and provides the advantage of directly manipulating the UI.
frontmatter.title: Flutter and Firebase Setup for iOS App Dev

frontmatter.description: Notes on topics I've studied related to software development. SemVer(Semantic Versioning): A versioning system that assigns meaning using {major}.{minor}.{patch}. The most commonly used versioning approach. HeadVer: A versioning system using {head}.{yearweek}.{build}. It points out that SemVer is a versioning system for APIs, and instead focuses more on end-users.
frontmatter.title: Random Topics in Software Development

frontmatter.description: A summary of methods for deep learning model optimization. This is a work-in-progress post with content being added continuously. PCA: Given a mean-normalized matrix X, perform eigenvalue decomposition on the covariance matrix to obtain eigenvalues and eigenvectors, then represent data by keeping directions with large eigenvalues (i.e., directions of high variance)
frontmatter.title: Deep Learning Model Optimization

frontmatter.description: This post summarizes the Matryoshka Representation Learning paper, presented at NeurIPS 2022. When extracting embeddings for images or text, various pre-trained backbones are often used, and it is quite common to need to resize embeddings to a specific dimension depending on the use case.
frontmatter.title: Matryoshka Representation Learning

frontmatter.description: Notes on topics I've learned related to data engineering. ChatGPT was heavily used in the process, and for the sake of quick learning and time saving, some output was kept in its original form with only necessary edits applied before uploading. Parquet is an open-source columnar storage file format optimized for use with big data processing frameworks ...
frontmatter.title: Today I Learned (Data Engineering)

frontmatter.description: This post documents how to receive events via AWS Lambda and AWS SQS, and send alert notifications to Slack for specific events. Lambda development was done using the AWS Toolkit for VSCode, and Slack notifications were implemented using a Slack webhook bot.
frontmatter.title: Alert System with SQS, Lambda, and Slack

frontmatter.description: This post covers basic concepts related to Git workflows and how to set up CI/CD using GitHub Actions, along with workflow YAML files. This is a work-in-progress post, and new content is added as I learn more. I'm running personal tests in the yuhodots/workflows repository.
frontmatter.title: CI/CD with GitHub Actions

frontmatter.description: A comprehensive summary of fundamental React concepts, from basic elements to Redux and Hooks. JavaScript uses an imperative approach, while React uses a declarative approach. Define the desired state and React handles the appropriate updates. JSX is a syntax extension of JS used for UI representation in React.
frontmatter.title: React with Redux Basics

frontmatter.description: This post summarizes the paper On Train-Test Class Overlap and Detection for Image Retrieval, presented at CVPR 2024. The image retrieval task involves detecting a specific object in a given query image and then finding other images in an internal database that match that object.
frontmatter.title: On Train-Test Class Overlap for Image Retrieval

frontmatter.description: This post summarizes the Vision Transformer Adapter for Dense Predictions paper, selected as a Spotlight at ICLR 2023. Since the advent of ViT, transformer architectures have been actively adopted across various vision tasks. However, for dense prediction tasks such as object detection and image segmentation...
frontmatter.title: Vision Transformer Adapter for Dense Predictions

frontmatter.description: This post reviews the Data Engine and Appendix F sections of Meta AI Research's Segment Anything paper, examining the key considerations in automating the mask annotation process. In the very first stage, humans manually created mask annotations with partial assistance from the model.
frontmatter.title: Segment Anything Data Engine

frontmatter.description: This post summarizes the paper MobileInst: Video Instance Segmentation on the Mobile, presented at AAAI 2024. Performing instance segmentation on video data in mobile environments is important but remains a challenging task. Conducting mask inference for each instance on a per-frame basis...
frontmatter.title: MobileInst: Video Instance Segmentation on Mobile

frontmatter.description: This post summarizes the paper 'A Neural Corpus Indexer for Document Retrieval' presented at NeurIPS 2022. Document retrieval and ranking are key stages in web search engines. This paper proposes an end-to-end deep learning approach for document retrieval.
frontmatter.title: A Neural Corpus Indexer for Document Retrieval

frontmatter.description: When writing Airflow DAGs and connecting multiple tasks, the graph view can become quite complex. To address this, Airflow provides a feature called TaskGroup that helps organize tasks into hierarchical groups.
frontmatter.title: Dynamic Task Mapping with TaskGroups

frontmatter.description: I have been consistently recording my thoughts and emotions in a journal and revisiting them from time to time. But I don't think I've had many occasions where I actually expressed those thoughts and feelings outwardly. It's not that I carry particularly profound thoughts or complicated emotions, but I felt embarrassed to reveal them and was afraid of exposing my vulnerabilities and weaknesses.
frontmatter.title: An Early Retrospective of 2023

frontmatter.description: This post, written as part of the Pseudo Lab 7th cohort 'Writing About Latest Research Trends in a Fun and Easy Way' group, covers recent research trends on Weakly-Supervised Instance Segmentation. It is targeted at readers with a background in computer vision.
frontmatter.title: Weakly-Supervised Instance Segmentation

frontmatter.description: To make data-driven decisions or integrate AI algorithms and rules into services, it is necessary to process various data, store it in databases, and run queries. This post covers vector DB usage, basic SQL query syntax, and visualization methods.
frontmatter.title: Using Databases as an AI Engineer (feat. Qdrant)

frontmatter.description: This post summarizes the Visual Instruction Tuning paper, selected as an oral paper at NeurIPS 2023. Also known as LLaVA, it is the first paper in the multimodal learning field to attempt model training with an instruction tuning dataset consisting of image-text pairs.
frontmatter.title: LLaVA: Large Language and Vision Assistant

frontmatter.description: This post covers the fundamental concepts and sample code for using NVIDIA Triton Inference Server. Triton is an AI inference server built by NVIDIA. It provides various features for inference optimization and higher GPU utilization, and you can create inference APIs simply by storing models in a model repository.
frontmatter.title: NVIDIA Triton Inference Server

frontmatter.description: Apache Airflow is a workflow management system created by Airbnb. This post covers basic examples and usage of Airflow DAGs. You can install Airflow via pip in a local virtual environment, or pull and use the Airflow Docker image.
frontmatter.title: Apache Airflow DAGs

frontmatter.description: Brief notes on Poetry usage and pyproject.toml authoring for Python project packaging. In the past, creating packages involved using setuptools to write package info in setup.py and registering on PyPI, but recently the trend has shifted to using pyproject.toml.
frontmatter.title: Packaging Python Projects

frontmatter.description: A summary of the ICML 2023 oral paper, Test-Time Adaptation in the Presence of Open-World Data Shift. While TTA settings typically assume only covariate shift, this paper argues that real-world scenarios must also handle label shift.
frontmatter.title: TTA in the Presence of Open-World Data Shift

frontmatter.description: A summary of the paper 'Deformable DETR: Deformable Transformers for End-to-End Object Detection.' The authors propose a deformable attention module to speed up convergence and leverage multi-scale features to improve small object detection.
frontmatter.title: Deformable-DETR

frontmatter.description: A review of deep learning-based object detection algorithms. This post briefly summarizes well-known algorithms among two-stage and one-stage detectors. Comments on typos or inaccuracies are welcome.
frontmatter.title: An Overview of Object Detection

frontmatter.description: An overview of Active Learning, a method for discovering valuable data from an unlabeled data pool. Parts of Lilian Weng's blog post were translated with her permission; please refer to the original link for more details.
frontmatter.title: Learning with not Enough Data: Active Learning

frontmatter.description: A collection of ViT-related algorithms arranged in chronological order. Although the title says Variants of Vision Transformer, this post also covers papers that share the same architecture but differ only in training methods.
frontmatter.title: Variants of Vision Transformer

frontmatter.description: Brief notes on libraries and frameworks for AI model deployment. ONNX: Open Neural Network Exchange. A neural network model format developed by Facebook and Microsoft to make AI models built with different frameworks interoperable. TensorFlow, PyTorch, ...
frontmatter.title: Deep Learning Model Serving

frontmatter.description: Notes on handy features of tools frequently used by developers, from IDE keyboard shortcuts to vim, shell, and tmux. Also covers useful collaboration features and conventions. Well-written blog posts and resources are shared via links. Content is continuously updated as I learn new things.
frontmatter.title: Shortcuts for a Developer

frontmatter.description: A concise overview of key concepts in reinforcement learning, from table-based methods to deep RL. For study, I recommend reading Noh Seung-eun's 'Reinforcement Learning from Scratch' first, then Sutton's 'Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction.'
frontmatter.title: An Introduction to Reinforcement Learning

frontmatter.description: This post summarizes the Generalized Category Discovery paper presented at CVPR 2022. The paper defines a new task called GCD. Since the assumptions of the previously existing NCD were at odds with the real world, GCD was proposed to generalize the NCD setting.
frontmatter.title: Generalized Category Discovery

frontmatter.description: A summary of Transformers based on 'Formal Algorithms for Transformers' and 'Attention Is All You Need.' Attention is a mechanism that updates value vectors based on query and key vectors. Building on attention, the Transformer architecture was proposed in 2017.
frontmatter.title: Transformer Basics

frontmatter.description: This post organizes usage of MLOps tools with a focus on commands. Most content references official documentation, and any additional sources are mentioned in the text or added to references. Currently covers Docker, Kubernetes, MLflow, Kubeflow, and content is continuously updated as study progresses.
frontmatter.title: Machine Learning Operations (MLOps)

frontmatter.description: Mahalanobis distance is a useful tool for computing distances while taking data distribution into account. Understanding the formula requires knowledge of what the covariance matrix and its inverse represent, so this post explains and visualizes Mahalanobis distance step by step.
frontmatter.title: Distance Considering Data Distribution: Mahalanobis

frontmatter.description: This post reviews two Normalizer-Free ResNets papers published by DeepMind in 2021. While BatchNorm has several issues, it has been continuously used due to its significant impact on model performance. The papers propose methods that maintain performance even without BatchNorm.
frontmatter.title: Toward Removing Batch Normalization in ResNets

frontmatter.description: Notes on frequently used features from the Ray Tune documentation. This post is continuously updated as I study further, with hands-on code examples added along the way. Ray has three core elements: Task, Object, and Actor.
frontmatter.title: Hyperparameter Tuning with Ray Tune

frontmatter.description: toch.utils.data 공식 문서를 읽고 pytoch dataloder 사용법을 기록합니다. torch.utils.data.DataLoader class constructor arguments는 다음과 같습니다. PyTorch에서 지원하는 dataset의 타입은 두 가지가 있습니다. Map-style datasets과 Iterable-style datasets 입니다.
frontmatter.title: PyTorch Custom Dataloader

frontmatter.description: A curated list of papers in areas of interest. For recently read papers, key content is summarized in three lines. Papers without check marks are those that need to be revisited later. Papers are recorded in the fields of Few-shot Learning, Meta Learning, Incremental & Continual Learning, Self & Semi-supervised Learning, Reinforcement Learning, and Natural Language Processing.
frontmatter.title: Deep Learning Paper List

frontmatter.description: This post covers NeRF in the Wild (NeRF-W), presented as an oral paper at CVPR 2021. The paper points out that NeRF, an algorithm for the View Synthesis task, cannot handle in-the-wild data, and proposes an advanced version of NeRF to address this limitation.
frontmatter.title: NeRF in the Wild

frontmatter.description: This post summarizes the paper by Li, Xiaoya, et al., 'Dice loss for data-imbalanced NLP tasks,' presented at ACL 2020. Due to limited time for writing posts recently, this article focuses briefly on the key content.
frontmatter.title: Dice Loss for Data-imbalanced NLP Tasks

frontmatter.description: This post briefly covers classifier performance evaluation metrics including confusion matrix, precision, recall, sensitivity, F1-score, ROC curve, and AUC. Written with reference to Hands-On Machine Learning 2nd edition, along with additional sources listed in the references.
frontmatter.title: Performance Evaluation Metrics for Classifiers

frontmatter.description: This post summarizes continual lifelong learning, referencing Parisi, German I.'s Continual Lifelong Learning survey, Masana, Marc's Class Incremental Learning survey, and Liu's Learning on the Job paper.
frontmatter.title: Continual Lifelong Learning Overview

frontmatter.description: This post introduces the concept of batch normalization and discusses which normalization methods are appropriate in a meta-learning setting. Batch normalization is a technique proposed in a 2015 paper by Sergey Ioffe and Christian Szegedy. Using BatchNorm generally improves model performance.
frontmatter.title: Normalization in Meta-learning Settings

frontmatter.description: This is a collection of newly learned knowledge that feels too small for a dedicated post. Rather than recording daily study notes, I update content here irregularly whenever I learn something new. This post accumulates AI/ML-related technical knowledge. More recently written content is placed at the bottom.
frontmatter.title: Today I Learned (AI/ML)

frontmatter.description: This is a collection of newly learned knowledge that feels too small for a dedicated post. Rather than recording what I study every day, I update this page irregularly whenever I come across something noteworthy. This post accumulates CSE tech stack topics outside of AI/ML. More recently written entries are placed toward the bottom.
frontmatter.title: Today I Learned (CSE)

frontmatter.description: This post reviews the Bootstrap your own latent paper by the DeepMind team, published at NeurIPS 2020. Training AI models requires large amounts of data, which includes not only the data itself but also labels to verify whether the predictions are correct.
frontmatter.title: Bootstrap Your Own Latent

frontmatter.description: This post reviews the paper Learning to Propagate Labels: Transductive Propagation Network for Few-shot Learning, published at ICLR 2019. I originally prepared this as a script for a lab seminar and decided to turn it into a blog post as well.
frontmatter.title: Transductive Propagation Network

frontmatter.description: This post translates the TensorFlow official documentation for tf.gradient and compute_gradient, and summarizes their differences based on Stack Overflow references. tf.gradient computes the symbolic derivative of ys with respect to xs.
frontmatter.title: tf.gradient and tf.train.Optimizer.compute_gradient

frontmatter.description: We installed a GeForce RTX 3090 in our lab workstation server. After the GPU was physically installed by a technician, I set up the experiment environment on Ubuntu 18.04 x86_64. Here I share the steps I followed.
frontmatter.title: Setting Up a Deep Learning Environment with RTX 3090

frontmatter.description: Our lab uses Ubuntu 18.04 OS as a machine learning workstation, and I'm compiling frequently used commands for server management and day-to-day work below. I keep adding new content whenever I learn something new or find something worth sharing.
frontmatter.title: Linux Basic Commands

frontmatter.description: Through this short post, I'd like to look back on my blogging journey so far and recalibrate the direction going forward. It has already been a year and four months since I first created this blog. At the time, I was interested in web development and didn't plan to pursue it as a career, but I wanted to study it consistently for at least a year. So I set up this blog as a way to organize what I learned after taking a basic web development course, and the early posts were mostly about following along with and summarizing lecture content.
frontmatter.title: Future Direction of This Blog

frontmatter.description: A brief summary of definitions and properties based on Wikipedia and the Deep Learning Book, organized for reference. Expectation is the sum over all events of the product of a random variable and its probability - it can be thought of as the mean of a probabilistic event, which can be treated as the population mean.
frontmatter.title: Expectation, Variance, and Covariance

frontmatter.description: This chapter primarily covers concepts related to statistics. Understanding the following statistics concepts can be useful when analyzing generalization, underfitting, and overfitting.
frontmatter.title: Estimators, Bias, Variance, and Standard Error

frontmatter.description: This post is a translated summary based on the Deep Learning book by Ian Goodfellow. Please note that some content may have been added or omitted based on my personal understanding. I welcome any corrections on inaccuracies.
frontmatter.title: KL Divergence and Cross-Entropy

frontmatter.description: In February 2019, I started a project with friends from the campus Enactus club, and we wrapped it up in June 2020. At this point, having just finished the project, I'd like to document the past year and a half below. Our project is called 'U-IT-DA,' a name created to signify connecting students of UNIST, the university I attend, through our web service.
frontmatter.title: Wrapping Up the Year-Long U-IT-DA Project

frontmatter.description: When working with image data in deep learning, it is useful to understand the modes associated with different image file formats. In this post, we will explore the commonly used image data file formats and modes.
frontmatter.title: Image Data Modes and Image File Formats

frontmatter.description: Occasionally I need to use TensorFlow version 1 instead of 2.0, so I'm posting a rough guide on how to use it. My local machine has TensorFlow 2.0 installed, but when I need version 1, I use Google Colab. (Apparently, Colab will soon replace TensorFlow version 1 with 2.0.)
frontmatter.title: An Introduction to TensorFlow 1.0

frontmatter.description: The backend part of web development is responsible for fetching data from the database and receiving/sending data to the frontend. The most basic way to perform these tasks is through HTTP GET and POST methods. Postman is a program that dramatically improves workflow efficiency. Let's explore how to obtain an access token and log in using Postman.
frontmatter.title: How to Send OAuth 2.0 Auth Requests with Postman

frontmatter.description: Before building a project yourself, let's examine someone else's project directory structure to get a sense of how to organize directories. In my case, I referenced the directory structure of a friend who had studied React before me.
frontmatter.title: Understanding React Project Structure

frontmatter.description: This post is based on the orientation lecture from the Numerical Analysis course I took last year. The MATLAB interface typically consists of a directory on the left, a script editor in the center, a workspace (variables and values) on the right, and a command window at the bottom. On Mac, use the Command key instead of Ctrl.
frontmatter.title: MATLAB Basic Commands

frontmatter.description: React components have three main lifecycle phases that we can monitor and manipulate. In this post, we will study React's lifecycle phases: Mounting, Updating, and Unmounting. Before diving in, let's first learn about the DOM, a prerequisite concept.
frontmatter.title: React Lifecycle

frontmatter.description: Install the create-react-app module globally. Create a project using the create-react-app command. Run the app and verify it works at localhost:3000. The most fundamental concept in React is the component.
frontmatter.title: React Basics: Components

frontmatter.description: When developing web applications that various users access, there are time-related tasks such as post creation time, login time, and comment timestamps. While you can use built-in DB functions like NOW(), let's explore the moment.js module that provides intuitive formatting and outputs the time you want.
frontmatter.title: Working with Time Using the moment.js Library

frontmatter.description: Since we need to use React, let's install it and create a React project in the desired directory. To verify the project works correctly, run npm start in the terminal from the directory where App.js is located.
frontmatter.title: Displaying FullCalendar Events with React

frontmatter.description: Before diving into detailed usage, let's start by displaying FullCalendar on the screen. This post explains how to display FullCalendar using script tags. If you want to use an ES6 build system instead, please refer to the official documentation.
frontmatter.title: Displaying FullCalendar Events with Script

frontmatter.description: While working on the backend with Node.js, MySQL, AWS EC2, S3, Nginx, and other tools, I occasionally encounter the error log 'Error: Connection lost: The server closed the connection.' which causes the website to become inaccessible. I am documenting what I learned through research about why this error occurs and how to resolve it.
frontmatter.title: Resolving Connection Lost: The Server Closed the Connection Error

frontmatter.description: The most fundamental things when using Node.js Express are the req object, res object, and the express() function. Since I tend to forget these concepts frequently, I put together a summary referencing the official Express website. req.body holds POST data because the request information is contained in the body of the request rather than in the URL.
frontmatter.title: req and res Objects, Comparing res.send and res.json

frontmatter.description: When starting to study frontend development, ECMAScript, TypeScript, and React are among the most frequently mentioned topics. Wikipedia describes ECMAScript as a standardized scripting programming language defined in the ECMA-262 technical specification by Ecma International.
frontmatter.title: What are ECMAScript, TypeScript, and React?

frontmatter.description: In the previous post, we learned how to obtain SSL certification, connect it to a domain, and force HTTP to redirect to HTTPS within app.js in Node.js. This time, we will explore how to implement HTTPS redirection in Nginx instead.
frontmatter.title: HTTPS Redirection via Nginx Configuration

frontmatter.description: About four days ago, I had just obtained an ACM certificate on AWS and enabled HTTPS access for my website, which made me quite proud. While happily working on the backend with that sense of accomplishment, my website suddenly spat out an nginx 502: Bad Gateway error and became completely inaccessible.
frontmatter.title: My Recent 502 Bad Gateway Error and Lessons Learned

frontmatter.description: It feels like I just started blogging, yet August is already here. When I first began, I was determined to diligently post about what I studied, but I also worried that after two or three months I'd get bored and my posting frequency would drop drastically. Yet here I am, still writing regularly, which tells me I've been more consistent than I expected.
frontmatter.title: Why You Should Blog

frontmatter.description: The 's' in https stands for Secure Socket Layer, commonly abbreviated as SSL or TLS (Transport Layer Security). You need SSL/TLS certification to make your website more secure against attackers.
frontmatter.title: What is AWS Certificate Manager?

frontmatter.description: In the previous AWS ACM post, we learned how to obtain an SSL certificate through ACM for a server using Route 53 and AWS EC2. In this post, we will explore how to apply the issued SSL certificate to our server relatively easily using ELB.
frontmatter.title: Applying SSL Certificates with ELB

frontmatter.description: Let's implement a system using Node-Express and MySQL where only properly logged-in users can write posts, and only the owner of a post can edit or delete it. I created a function that checks whether the request object contains a user value, and another that verifies whether the logged-in user matches the post author.
frontmatter.title: Writing Posts and Authentication with MySQL

frontmatter.description: I'm using express-session and passport.js (local strategy) to implement login, registration, and authentication features. This requires creating a session store, and I plan to use MySQL as the session store.
frontmatter.title: Authentication with MySQL and Passport

frontmatter.description: S3 is one of the services provided by Amazon Web Services. It stands for Simple Storage Service. In other words, it's a service for storing information — or more simply put, a file storage service.
frontmatter.title: Using S3 SDK in Node.js

frontmatter.description: To build a bulletin board that supports reading, creating, editing, and deleting posts using a MySQL database, user-submitted text must be sent to the database server, which requires pre-modeling the table structure.
frontmatter.title: Database Modeling

frontmatter.description: Since Express does not natively provide the ability to save user-uploaded files to a desired directory on the server, we need to install a module called multer to handle this.
frontmatter.title: Using the Multer Module for File Uploads in Express

frontmatter.description: When you look at the app.js file of a web application project generated by Express-generator, you'll find about 40 lines of code. Today, let's study what each of these lines means.
frontmatter.title: Understanding the app.js Generated by Express-generator

frontmatter.description: If you want to manage your web application with a version control system, you can use the git clone command in your desired directory to connect with GitHub.
frontmatter.title: Connecting EC2 Instances with GitHub Private Repos

frontmatter.description: The configuration for how nginx operates is described in configuration files with the .conf extension. If installed via compilation, it's located at /usr/local/nginx/conf, and if installed on Ubuntu using apt-get, it's located at /etc/nginx.
frontmatter.title: Nginx Configuration

frontmatter.description: When using an AWS EC2 instance as a server, if you're not developing alone but working on a project with a team, all team members managing the server need access to that instance.
frontmatter.title: Access Control for AWS EC2 Resources

frontmatter.description: Making a web application work well is important, but even more important is protecting the personal information of users from being leaked by anonymous attackers. While there are countless attack methods for stealing personal information, let's look at one of the most fundamental protections: bcrypt.
frontmatter.title: Password Encryption with bcrypt

frontmatter.description: Instead of implementing authentication features like login from scratch, we can borrow authentication methods built by other development companies. This is where passport.js comes in. While there are many other middleware options, passport.js is the most widely used.
frontmatter.title: Implementing Authentication with Passport

frontmatter.description: A collection of useful resources and links on various topics, with brief comments where needed. This post was originally created in 2019 but has been partially reorganized, and I resumed updating it from 2023.
frontmatter.title: Reading List

frontmatter.description: To properly understand cookies, you first need to know about HTTP. HTTP stands for Hyper Text Transfer Protocol, a communication protocol governing the request/response relationship between servers and clients.
frontmatter.title: Cookies and HTTP

frontmatter.description: With the advent of cookies, the web gained the ability to remember previous communications. This laid the groundwork for personalization and authentication, but using cookies for authentication is extremely dangerous. This led to the need for a different approach, and sessions were born.
frontmatter.title: Express-Session

frontmatter.description: If you were to pick two important features of Express, one would be routing and the other would be middleware. Middleware is one way to use components made by others for implementing your web page. You can find various middleware on the Express website.
frontmatter.title: Express Middleware

frontmatter.description: There are various frameworks based on Node.js, but Express is the most commonly used one among them. Let's build a web page using Express. To do this, you'll need to have basic programs like npm, Node.js, and PM2 installed first.
frontmatter.title: Setting Up and Running Express

frontmatter.description: It's time to learn about Create / Read / Update / Delete that was introduced in the previous post. Please remember that Create and Read are the most important among them, and let's see how they are implemented in MySQL below.
frontmatter.title: MySQL CRUD and JOIN

frontmatter.description: Spreadsheets and databases can both display information in tabular form. They also offer convenient features like sorting by ID or by date. However, while spreadsheets control data through button clicks, databases control data using a language called SQL.
frontmatter.title: MySQL Basics

frontmatter.description: When there are multiple tasks, completing one before starting the next, and then completing that one before starting the following one, is called 'synchronous.' In contrast, when there are multiple tasks and you delegate a time-consuming one to someone else, have them notify you when it's done, and immediately start the next task yourself, that is called 'asynchronous.'
frontmatter.title: Synchronous and Asynchronous, PM2

frontmatter.description: The web can broadly be divided into web browsers and web servers. When you enter an address in a web browser to make a request to a server, the web server finds and responds with the appropriate content. Node.js has a built-in web server, so it can also serve as a web server.
frontmatter.title: Node.js Basics

frontmatter.description: An important aspect of JavaScript in web programming is that it enables interaction with the user. A web browser has no ability to change itself once it renders on screen. However, using JavaScript can compensate for this limitation to a considerable extent.
frontmatter.title: JavaScript Basics (script, event, console)
